commentaries.author: Augustine of Hippo
commentaries.content: 1 Esdras Wisdom of Solomon Wisdom of Sirach Epistle of Jeremiah Prayer of Manasseh The preparations of the heart belong to man, but the answer of the tongue, is from the LORD. Augustine of Hippo Assuredly, as to what is written, “The preparation of the heart is man’s part, and the answer of the tongue is from the Lord,” they are misled by an imperfect understanding, so as to think that to prepare the heart—that is, to begin good—pertains to people without the aid of God’s grace. Be it far from the children of promise thus to understand it. Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: It is the part of man That is, a man should prepare in his heart and soul what he is to say; but after all, it must be the Lord that must govern his tongue, to speak to the purpose. Not that we can think any thing of good without God's grace: but after that we have (with God's grace) thought and prepared within our souls what we would speak; if God does not govern our tongue, we shall not succeed in what we speak. (Challoner) He will put into our mouths what we have to say to persecutors, Luke xxi. He often causes us to utter the reverse of what we intended, as Balaam did, Numbers xxiii. (Menochius) The fairest prospects miscarry without God's blessing. The enemies of grace would infer from this text, that the beginning of salvation depends on free-will. Augustine (con. ) has solidly refuted them, and Solomon does not mean that man acts alone, chap. , (Septuagint) John xv. , and 2 Corinthians iii. "Man "says St. Augustine, "does no good things, which God does not cause him to perform. "(Calmet) The Scripture cannot contradict itself. A fresh grace is requisite to execute what God has enabled us to devise, ver. (Worthington).

commentaries.author: Richard Challoner
commentaries.content: 1 Esdras Wisdom of Solomon Wisdom of Sirach Epistle of Jeremiah Prayer of Manasseh The preparations of the heart belong to man, but the answer of the tongue, is from the LORD. It is the part of man: That is, a man should prepare in his heart and soul what he is to say: but after all, it must be the Lord that must govern his tongue, to speak to the purpose. Not that we can think any thing of good without God's grace; but that after we have (with God's grace) thought and prepared within our souls what we would speak, if God does not govern our tongue, we shall not succeed in what we speak.
